title: Quantum Radar Cross Section with two-photon entangled states
authors: Sunghwa Kang, Jihwan Kim, Zaeill Kim, Duk Y. Kim, Yong Sup Ihn, Su-Yong Lee, Sean Crowe, Stefan Evans, Marcio de Andrade, Joanna Ptasinski
abstract: We study two-photon entangled states for quantum radar cross section (QRCS), which is an extension of a single-photon QRCS formula. Since signal-idler entanglement does not provide any enhancement of the QRCS [Brandsema's PhD Thesis (2017)], we focus on signal-signal entanglement and derive the corresponding biphoton QRCS. We show that it can provide an enhancement over the single-photon QRCS and two-photon separable QRCS, where the performance is evaluated for various two-dimensional target geometries in monostatic/bistatic configurations. Furthermore, using the double-Gaussian approximation, we derive QRCS formula for biphoton states with arbitrary degree of entanglement and compute the resulting scattering patterns.
summary: This paper theoretically analyzes the Quantum Radar Cross Section (QRCS) using two-photon entangled states. It shows that utilizing signal-signal entanglement provides an enhancement in detection capability compared to classical or single-photon sources. The results are demonstrated across various 2D targets, confirming the utility of quantum correlations for advanced sensing applications.
main_problem: The study investigates how using two-photon entangled states can enhance the Quantum Radar Cross Section (QRCS) compared to single-photon or separable classical sources.
main_result: Two-photon entanglement, particularly in signal-signal correlations, provides a measurable enhancement in QRCS, especially visible in the side lobes of the scattering pattern. Furthermore, non-maximal entanglement can sometimes outperform maximally entangled states for specific geometries and parameters.
method: The authors derive the biphoton QRCS using scattering theory applied to two-photon entangled states, employing the double-Gaussian approximation for arbitrary entanglement degrees.
model_or_system: The model involves quantum radar detection where photons scatter off various two-dimensional target geometries (square, circle, triangle) in both monostatic and bistatic configurations. The analysis is based on atom-photon scattering principles.
key_observables: Quantum Radar Cross Section ($\sigma_Q$), which quantifies the enhancement over single-photon or separable QRCS.
important_parameters: Entanglement parameters ($\sigma, \mu$), target size ($L$), wavelength ($\lambda$), and the degree of entanglement quantified by the Schmidt number $K$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the double-Gaussian approximation for the incident state and assumes atoms have randomly oriented dipole moments. A key distinction is made between indistinguishable (formulated) and distinguishable photons.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate various 2D target geometries (Square, Circle, Triangle) and present comparative plots of Monostatic and Bistatic QRCS results for different entanglement levels and targets.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the QRCS using two-photon entangled states, deriving the general formula via the double-Gaussian approximation, and then numerically evaluating this enhancement across various target geometries and entanglement regimes in both monostatic and bistatic setups.
why_interesting: This work bridges quantum optics and applied physics by demonstrating a tangible advantage of quantum correlations (entanglement) for sensing applications like radar, which is highly relevant to quantum information processing and advanced measurement techniques.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Targets with two-dimensional geometries. L denotes the length of a side of a square, the diameter of a circle, the base and height of a triangle. We set L = 1m.
FIG. 2. Monostatic QRCS for a square plate target consisting of 961 atoms arranged on a uniform grid.
FIG. 3. Monostatic QRCS for a circular plate target consist- ing of 709 atoms.
FIG. 4. Monostatic QRCS for a triangular plate target con- sisting of 462 atoms.
FIG. 6. Bistatic QRCS for a circular plate target at an inci- dent angle θi = 85◦.
